items.title: Re: CVE-2026-9641: Crypt::PBKDF2 versions before 0.261630 for Perl have a weak default algorithm and number of iterations
items.summary:
Posted by Harry Sintonen on Jun 14 No it doesn't. Since with HMAC the collisions are not a problem(*),  
2^128 is plenty large enough keyspace against bruteforce attacks. Even  
combining the insane compute power that is in things like the current  
cryptocurrency systems it would still take way longer than the current  
age of the universe to bruteforce. 
 
*)  https://eprint.iacr.org/2006/043.pdf  
 
   Regards,

items.title: Re: CVE-2026-9641: Crypt::PBKDF2 versions before 0.261630 for Perl have a weak default algorithm and number of iterations
items.summary:
Posted by Jacob Bachmeyer on Jun 14 Does the shorter output length (128 bits for MD5; 160 bits for SHA-1)  
cause problems?  Has the general advance of computing power caught up to  
HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA1, or do they remain secure? (Similar to how DES  
remains unbroken in the cryptanalytic sense, but its 56-bit keyspace is  
now vulnerable to brute force.) 
 
-- Jacob

Peter.
